findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Missing allowed-tools restriction for Write tool
findings.description: The skill declares `allowed-tools: [Read, Write, Bash(ls *), Bash(mkdir *)]` but the instructions explicitly use Write operations (writing to `~/.claude/channels/telegram/approved/<senderId>` and writing `access.json`). While Write is declared, the Bash restrictions are overly specific and may not cover all intended operations. This is a minor inconsistency in tool declaration.
findings.evidence: allowed-tools includes Bash(ls *) and Bash(mkdir *) but instructions use mkdir -p and write operations
findings.remediation: Clarify allowed-tools to accurately reflect all operations performed by the skill

findings.title: Potential tool poisoning via file-based state management
findings.description: The skill reads and writes `~/.claude/channels/telegram/access.json` which could be modified by other skills or processes. While this is by design, it creates a potential attack surface where a malicious skill could corrupt the access control state. The skill correctly handles missing files but does not validate JSON structure integrity.
findings.evidence: State lives in `~/.claude/channels/telegram/access.json` - shared file system
findings.remediation: Consider adding JSON schema validation when reading the state file

findings.title: Missing input validation for senderId
findings.description: The skill explicitly states 'Sender IDs are opaque strings (Telegram numeric user IDs). Don't validate format.' This could allow path traversal or injection attacks if senderId contains special characters when used in file paths (writing to `~/.claude/channels/telegram/approved/<senderId>`).
findings.evidence: Sender IDs are used as filenames without validation
findings.remediation: Sanitize senderId to prevent path traversal (e.g., strip slashes, dots, and special characters)

findings.title: Potential command injection via groupId parameter
findings.description: The `group add` and `group rm` commands use groupId directly in file operations. If groupId contains shell metacharacters, it could potentially be exploited when used with Bash operations.
findings.evidence: groupId is used in file operations without sanitization
findings.remediation: Sanitize groupId to prevent path traversal and injection attacks
